title: Soil Horizons and Texture Explorer
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim with split-screen soil profile horizons and texture triangle for classifying soil types.

learningObjectives: Classify soil types using the texture triangle and identify soil horizons in a profile diagram
Describe the composition and ecological role of each soil horizon (O, A, B, C, R)
Explain how soil texture affects drainage, nutrient retention, and plant growth
Connect soil properties to ecosystem services and agricultural productivity
prerequisites: Basic understanding of weathering and erosion processes
Understanding that soil is a non-renewable resource on human timescales

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Split-screen interactive exploration with hover-to-learn and slider-based classification
keyQuestions: What is the ecological role of each soil horizon?
How does soil texture affect water drainage and nutrient retention?
Why is the A horizon (topsoil) considered a non-renewable resource?
What soil texture is ideal for agriculture and why?
commonMisconceptions: Soil is just dirt with no internal structure or organization
Sandy soil is always better because it drains well, ignoring nutrient loss
Soil forms quickly and is easily replaceable after erosion
assessmentOpportunities: Students classify soil samples given sand/silt/clay percentages using the texture triangle
Students match soil horizons to their ecological roles from memory
Students recommend soil amendments for a given agricultural scenario
